one_line_takeaway: Arbiter applies a two-phase LLM-scouring-plus-formal-rule methodology to find internal contradictions and design-seam bugs in three commercial coding-agent system prompts, showing that prompt architecture predicts failure class (not severity) and that no single LLM scourer finds the same set of issues as another.
key_finding: Prompt architecture (monolithic/flat/modular) correlates strongly with the class of interference bug found, but not with severity: Claude Code's monolithic 1,490-line prompt produces growth-level contradictions at subsystem boundaries (e.g., a TodoWrite mandate 'ALWAYS use' directly contradicting commit/PR-workflow prohibitions 'NEVER use TodoWrite' -- 4 critical contradictions, plus 13 scope-overlap duplications of the same constraint worded differently in 2-3 places); Codex CLI's flat 298-line prompt trades capability for consistency and yields mostly structural/cosmetic findings (identity confusion between 'GPT-5.2' and 'Codex CLI', an empty section header, a leaked citation-suppression detail); Gemini CLI's modular, runtime-composed 245-line prompt produces design-level bugs at composition seams, most notably a structural data-loss bug where saved user preferences via save_memory have no corresponding field in the history-compression schema and are therefore guaranteed to be deleted on compression -- a finding the authors say was later independently corroborated when Google filed and patched a related P0 infinite-loop bug (issue #16213, PR #16914) without touching the underlying schema gap. Undirected multi-model scouring produced 152 total findings across vendors (116/Claude Code over 10 passes, 15/Codex over 2 passes, 21/Gemini over 3 passes) at a combined measured API cost of $0.263 (Claude Opus pass excluded), and different models were shown to find categorically different finding types rather than overlapping/redundant ones (e.g., Kimi K2.5 concentrated on economic/resource-exhaustion findings, GLM 4.7 on data integrity, Grok 4.1 on permission-schema gaps), with 107 unique self-invented categories across 116 Claude Code findings.
core_contribution: The paper introduces Arbiter, a two-phase framework for finding 'interference' (internal contradictions, redundancies, ambiguities) in LLM coding-agent system prompts, treated as untested software artifacts. Phase 1 ('directed evaluation') decomposes a prompt into classified blocks (tier/category/modality/scope) and checks block pairs against five formal rules (mandate-prohibition conflict, scope-overlap redundancy, priority-marker ambiguity, implicit dependency, verbatim duplication), three of which are cheap structural/Python predicates and two of which require an LLM call. Phase 2 ('undirected scouring') sends the prompt to a sequence of different LLMs with a deliberately vague 'note what you find interesting' prompt, passing each model the accumulated findings of prior passes, and stops after three consecutive models decline to continue. The authors also build a vendor-neutral prompt AST parser/differ (Document->Section->{Paragraph,Directive,List,CodeBlock,Metadata} with semantic-role and channel tagging, plus structural hashing) to give quantitative structural profiles and version-diffing. The framework is applied to the system prompts of three commercial coding agents: Claude Code (Anthropic), Codex CLI (OpenAI), and Gemini CLI (Google).
threat_model: Not an adversarial/attacker threat model. The paper explicitly distinguishes itself from prompt-injection research (Section 2.2): it analyzes a system prompt for internal self-contradiction, not for susceptibility to adversarial external inputs, and states 'a system prompt can be perfectly secure against injection yet internally contradictory.' The closest thing to a security-relevant finding is one scourer-discovered category, 'security architecture gaps (system-reminder trust as injection surface)', which is listed as one of several undirected-scouring finding categories but is not elaborated with a concrete exploit or PoC in the body text extracted. Two findings are flagged in the paper's own 'Responsible Disclosure' section: (1) Gemini CLI's save_memory user preferences are structurally guaranteed to be deleted during history-compression because the compression schema has no field for them, and (2) Claude Code's Task tool's 'Tools: *' specification includes the Task tool itself, enabling unbounded recursive agent spawning (noted as a known/accepted architectural choice rather than a demonstrated exploit).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) static text analysis only -- a detected contradiction may or may not manifest as an actual runtime failure, and the paper leans on a separate cited study (Gloaguen et al. 2026, AGENTS.md) for indirect runtime corroboration rather than its own runtime experiments; (2) scourer findings are LLM-generated and can be fabricated, mitigated only by multi-model cross-checking and unspecified 'human review'; (3) the 56-block/21-pattern directed-analysis ground truth exists for Claude Code only, not for Codex or Gemini CLI; (4) the Gemini CLI prompt was not captured as-is but reconstructed/composed by the authors from TypeScript render functions with all feature flags enabled, so it may not match any single real deployed configuration; (5) the $0.27 total cost figure excludes the Claude Opus pass, billed via subscription, making it an explicit lower bound; (6) the three-vendor architectural taxonomy (monolith/flat/modular -> failure class) is described by the authors themselves as 'a strong correlation in this sample, not a universal law' given only three data points.

datasets_used: Claude Code system prompt v2.1.50 (1,490 lines, 78K chars, extracted from published npm package)
Claude Code system prompt v2.1.71 (used only for AST version-diff comparison)
Codex CLI system prompt (298 lines, 22K chars, plaintext Markdown from open-source repo)
Gemini CLI system prompt (245 lines, 27K chars, reconstructed by authors from TypeScript render functions with all feature flags enabled)
49 instrumented-proxy sessions (March 3-7, 2026) used to check per-session invariance of prompt structure
quantitative_results: Directed analysis of Claude Code: 56 hand-classified blocks -> 21 hand-labeled interference patterns (4 critical direct contradictions, 13 scope overlaps, 2 priority ambiguities, 2 implicit dependencies); 20/21 (95%) judged statically detectable
Pre-filtering reduced Claude Code block-pair-rule search space from ~15,680 to 100-200 evaluated pairs
Undirected scourer findings: Claude Code 116 (10 passes, 10 models, stopped on 3 consecutive 'no'); Codex CLI 15 (2 passes); Gemini CLI 21 (3 passes)

Scourer severity distribution -- Claude Code: curious 34 (29%), notable 36 (31%), concerning 34 (29%), alarming 12 (10%); Codex CLI: curious 3 (20%), notable 7 (47%), concerning 5 (33%), alarming 0 (0%); Gemini CLI: curious 4 (19%), notable 9 (43%), concerning 6 (29%), alarming 2 (10%)
Total measured API cost across all three vendor analyses: $0.263 (Claude Code $0.236, Codex CLI $0.012, Gemini CLI $0.014), cost per finding $0.002

Per-model API cost (OpenRouter, 26 calls total): Kimi K2.5 $0.054, DeepSeek R1 $0.054, Qwen3-235B $0.053, GLM 4.7 $0.039, Grok 4.1 Fast $0.016, Llama 4 Maverick $0.015, DeepSeek V3.2 $0.012, MiniMax M2.5 $0.012, Gemini 2.0 Flash $0.005, GPT-OSS 120B $0.003

AST structural profiles (nodes / max depth / sections / top-level directives / unclassified nodes): Claude v2.1.50 80/2/0/7/23(29%); Claude v2.1.71 110/4/12/2/1(1%); Codex CLI 183/5/16/0/13(7%); Gemini CLI 151/4/21/0/1(1%)
Claude Code v2.1.50->v2.1.71 evolution: depth 2->4, sections 0->12, tool_schema channel share 35%->13%, memory channel emerged at 25% (28 nodes), unclassified nodes 29%->1%
Category explosion: 107 unique self-invented finding categories across 116 Claude Code scourer findings

Claude Code scourer convergence was non-monotonic: pass 6 (Llama 4 Maverick) produced only 5 new findings, pass 7 (MiniMax M2.5) produced a surge of 20 new findings, before pass 8-10 (Qwen3-235B, GLM 4.7, GPT-OSS 120B) voted to stop after producing 3, 14, and 8 findings respectively
baselines_compared: No external baseline systems are compared; the paper's own directed-evaluation hand-labeled ground truth (21 patterns) serves as the reference against which the value of the undirected scouring phase is argued (scourer finds categories, e.g. security/economic/operational, for which no directed rule exists)

Single-model vs multi-model scouring is the paper's internal ablation-style comparison: the authors argue multi-model scouring finds categorically different findings than any single model, evidenced by per-model characteristic-focus differences (e.g., Kimi K2.5 economic/resource findings vs. Grok 4.1 permission-schema findings) and by 9/10 models converging on 'security & trust' as a shared category while other categories are dominated by 1-2 models
